nodes.name: World Religions & Religious History
nodes.description: Major world religions, founders, schisms, and sacred sites

items.prompt: Which religion, with approximately 2.6 billion adherents worldwide, is centered on the teachings of a 1st-century figure from Nazareth and uses the Bible as its sacred text?
items.explanation:
World's largest religion with ~2.6 billion adherents (~31% of global population)
Founded on the teachings of Jesus of Nazareth, believed by followers to be the Son of God and the Messiah
Sacred text is the Bible (Old Testament + New Testament); central event is the crucifixion and resurrection of Jesus
Three major branches: Roman Catholicism (~1.26 billion), Protestantism, and Eastern Orthodoxy
Originated in 1st-century Roman Palestine; became the Roman Empire's state religion under Theodosius I in 380 CE
Central sacraments include baptism and the Eucharist (Communion)
The cross is its primary symbol; Christmas and Easter are its most widely observed holidays
items.cardFront: Largest world religion, ~2.6 billion, Bible, Jesus of Nazareth

items.prompt: Which religion, founded in 7th-century Arabia, requires its followers to observe five core duties including daily prayer, fasting during Ramadan, and pilgrimage to a holy city?
items.explanation:
World's second-largest religion with ~2 billion adherents (~24% of global population)
Founded by the Prophet Muhammad in 7th-century Mecca and Medina, modern-day Saudi Arabia
Sacred text is the Quran, believed to be the word of God revealed to Muhammad through the angel Gabriel
Five Pillars: Shahada (declaration of faith), Salah (prayer 5x daily), Zakat (almsgiving), Sawm (fasting during Ramadan), Hajj (pilgrimage to Mecca)
Two major branches: Sunni (~85%) and Shia (~15%), split over succession after Muhammad's death in 632 CE
Fastest-growing major religion worldwide by population growth rate
~62% of adherents live in the Asia-Pacific region, from Turkey to Indonesia
items.cardFront: Second-largest religion, ~2 billion, Quran, Five Pillars

items.prompt: Which religion, the oldest of the major world faiths with roots extending over 4,000 years, has no single founder and reveres texts called the Vedas and Upanishads?
items.explanation:
World's third-largest religion with ~1.2 billion adherents (~15% of global population)
Oldest major living religion, with roots in the Indus Valley civilization dating back over 4,000 years
No single founder, no single scripture, and no single set of teachings — evolved organically over millennia
Sacred texts include the Vedas (oldest, c. 1500 BCE), Upanishads, Bhagavad Gita, and the epics Mahabharata and Ramayana
Core concepts: dharma (duty), karma (action and consequence), samsara (cycle of rebirth), and moksha (liberation)
Major deities include Brahma (creator), Vishnu (preserver), and Shiva (destroyer) — the Trimurti
The vast majority of adherents live in India and Nepal
items.cardFront: Oldest major religion, ~1.2 billion, Vedas, no single founder

items.prompt: Which religion, founded by a prince-turned-ascetic in 5th-century BCE India, teaches the Four Noble Truths and the Eightfold Path as the way to end suffering?
items.explanation:
Founded by Siddhartha Gautama (the Buddha) in northeastern India, c. 5th century BCE
Core teaching: the Four Noble Truths — life involves suffering (dukkha), caused by desire, which can be ended by following the Eightfold Path
Eightfold Path: right view, thought, speech, action, livelihood, effort, mindfulness, and concentration
Two major branches: Theravada (dominant in Southeast Asia) and Mahayana (dominant in East Asia); Vajrayana (Tibetan) is a subset of Mahayana
Siddhartha was a prince who renounced luxury after witnessing old age, sickness, and death — attained enlightenment under the Bodhi Tree at age 35
Only major world religion whose global population declined between 2010 and 2020, per Pew Research
Approximately 324 million adherents worldwide as of 2020
items.cardFront: Founded by Siddhartha Gautama, Four Noble Truths, Eightfold Path

items.prompt: Which religion, the oldest of the Abrahamic faiths with roots stretching back over 3,500 years, uses the Torah as its most sacred text and traces its origin to a covenant between God and Abraham?
items.explanation:
Oldest Abrahamic religion, with origins dating back over 3,500 years
Approximately 15 million adherents worldwide, primarily in Israel and the United States
Sacred texts: the Torah (first five books of the Hebrew Bible), the Tanakh, and the Talmud (rabbinic commentary)
Central to its theology: one God, the covenant with Abraham, the Exodus from Egypt under Moses, and the giving of the Law at Mount Sinai
Three major branches: Orthodox, Conservative, and Reform
The Sabbath (Shabbat) runs from Friday evening to Saturday evening; major holidays include Passover, Yom Kippur, and Rosh Hashanah
Gave rise to both Christianity and Islam — all three are called Abrahamic religions because they trace spiritual lineage to Abraham
items.cardFront: Oldest Abrahamic faith, ~15 million, Torah, covenant with Abraham

items.prompt: What was the 1054 event in which Cardinal Humbert excommunicated the Patriarch of Constantinople at Hagia Sophia, permanently splitting the Church into Western and Eastern branches?
items.explanation:
Formal split in 1054 between the Roman Catholic Church (West) and Eastern Orthodox Church (East)
Triggered when Cardinal Humbert placed a bull of excommunication on the altar of Hagia Sophia in Constantinople on July 16, 1054
Patriarch Michael Cerularius responded by excommunicating the papal legates a week later
Key disputes: the Filioque clause (whether the Holy Spirit proceeds from the Father alone or Father and Son), use of leavened vs. unleavened bread in the Eucharist, and papal authority over Eastern churches
Centuries of political and theological tensions preceded the formal break, including the coronation of Charlemagne in 800 CE
Mutual excommunications were not lifted until 1965, when Pope Paul VI and Patriarch Athenagoras I nullified the 1054 anathemas
The schism remains unhealed — Roman Catholicism and Eastern Orthodoxy are still separate communions
items.cardFront: 1054, Catholic vs. Orthodox, Hagia Sophia excommunication

items.prompt: Which 16th-century religious movement began when an Augustinian monk posted 95 propositions on a church door in Wittenberg, challenging the sale of indulgences and papal authority?
items.explanation:
Launched on October 31, 1517, when Martin Luther posted his 95 Theses on the door of the Castle Church (Schlosskirche) in Wittenberg, Germany
Luther's central objection: the sale of indulgences (payments to reduce punishment for sins), particularly by Johann Tetzel on behalf of Pope Leo X
Core theological principle: salvation by faith alone (sola fide), not by works or purchased indulgences
Luther was excommunicated by Pope Leo X in 1521 and declared an outlaw at the Diet of Worms
Led to the founding of Lutheranism, Calvinism, Anglicanism, and other Protestant denominations
The printing press enabled rapid spread of Luther's ideas — the 95 Theses were reprinted across Germany within weeks
Triggered over a century of religious wars in Europe, including the Thirty Years' War (1618–1648)

items.prompt: Which 325 CE gathering, convened by Emperor Constantine I in modern-day Turkey, was the first ecumenical council and produced a foundational creed affirming the divinity of Jesus?
items.explanation:
First ecumenical council of the Christian Church, held in Nicaea (modern İznik, Turkey) in 325 CE
Convened by Roman Emperor Constantine I to resolve the Arian controversy — whether Christ was divine or a created being
Attended by approximately 318 bishops from across the Roman Empire
Produced the Nicene Creed, affirming Christ as homoousios (of one substance) with God the Father — rejecting Arianism as heresy
Also established a uniform date for Easter and issued early canon law
Nicaea was chosen for its proximity to the imperial capital of Nicomedia and its accessibility
The Nicene Creed remains the most widely accepted statement of faith across Catholic, Orthodox, and most Protestant churches
items.cardFront: 325 CE, Emperor Constantine, first ecumenical council, creed

items.prompt: Which series of religiously motivated military campaigns, launched by Pope Urban II in 1095 and lasting nearly two centuries, sought to recapture the Holy Land from Muslim control?
items.explanation:
Series of religious wars between Christians and Muslims, 1096–1291, primarily over control of the Holy Land
Initiated at the Council of Clermont in 1095 when Pope Urban II called for an armed pilgrimage to recapture Jerusalem
First Crusade (1096–1099) was the most successful — captured Jerusalem and established four Crusader states
Eight major Crusades total; the Fourth Crusade (1202–1204) infamously sacked Constantinople instead of reaching the Holy Land
Saladin (Salah ad-Din) recaptured Jerusalem in 1187, prompting the Third Crusade led by Richard the Lionheart, Philip II, and Frederick Barbarossa
Ended with the fall of Acre in 1291 — the last major Crusader stronghold in the Levant
Lasting effects: deepened Christian-Muslim hostility, introduced Europeans to Eastern goods and ideas, strengthened papal authority temporarily
items.cardFront: 1096–1291, Pope Urban II, Holy Land, religious wars

items.prompt: Which city in western Saudi Arabia, birthplace of the Prophet Muhammad, contains the Masjid al-Haram and draws millions of pilgrims annually for the hajj?
items.explanation:
Holiest city in Islam, located in the Hejaz region of western Saudi Arabia
Birthplace of the Prophet Muhammad (c. 570 CE) and the site of his first revelations
Home to the Masjid al-Haram (Grand Mosque), the largest mosque in the world and the most expensive building ever constructed
Contains the Kaaba, a cube-shaped stone structure that Muslims face during prayer (qibla) — believed to have been built by Abraham and Ishmael
Destination of the Hajj, one of the Five Pillars of Islam — every able Muslim must visit at least once in their lifetime
Over 5 million pilgrims perform the Hajj annually; the Kaaba is circled counterclockwise seven times (tawaf)
Non-Muslims are prohibited from entering the city
items.cardFront: Holiest city in Islam, Kaaba, Grand Mosque, Hajj destination

items.prompt: Which 109-acre city-state, the world's smallest independent country by both area and population, serves as the headquarters of the Roman Catholic Church and the residence of the Pope?
items.explanation:
World's smallest independent state at ~109 acres (44 hectares) with ~1,000 citizens
Enclave within Rome, Italy; established as a sovereign state by the Lateran Treaty of 1929 between Pope Pius XI and Mussolini's Italy
Seat of the Holy See — the central governing body of the Roman Catholic Church worldwide
Home to St. Peter's Basilica, the largest church in the world by interior measure, built over the traditional burial site of St. Peter the Apostle
Contains the Sistine Chapel, with Michelangelo's ceiling (completed 1512) and The Last Judgment (completed 1541)
Protected by the Swiss Guard since 1506 — the world's smallest and oldest standing army
Has its own post office, radio station, banking system, and railway station; Latin is one of its official languages
items.cardFront: Smallest country, Pope's residence, St. Peter's Basilica

items.prompt: At which site in Bihar, India, did a wandering ascetic attain enlightenment under a sacred fig tree around the 5th century BCE, marking the founding moment of a major world religion?
items.explanation:
Located in Bihar, India, on the banks of the Phalgu River — the site where Siddhartha Gautama attained enlightenment
Siddhartha meditated under a peepul tree (Ficus religiosa), which became known as the Bodhi Tree — a descendant still grows on the site
Home to the Mahabodhi Temple, one of the earliest brick Buddhist temples still standing in India, dating to the 5th–6th century CE
The first temple at the site was built by Emperor Ashoka in the 3rd century BCE
Designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2002
One of the four holiest sites in Buddhism, along with Lumbini (birthplace), Sarnath (first sermon), and Kushinagar (death)
Pilgrims and monks from around the world visit to meditate under the Bodhi Tree and at the Mahabodhi Temple
items.cardFront: Bihar, India; Bodhi Tree; Mahabodhi Temple; enlightenment site

items.prompt: Which ancient limestone wall in Jerusalem's Old City, a remnant of the Second Temple complex built by Herod the Great, is the holiest site where Jews are permitted to pray?
items.explanation:
Located in the Old City of Jerusalem — holiest place where Jews are permitted to pray
Not part of the Temple itself but a retaining wall built by Herod the Great (r. 37–4 BCE) to support the expansion of the Second Temple platform
The Second Temple was destroyed by the Romans in 70 CE; the wall is the closest accessible point to where the Holy of Holies once stood
Called the 'Wailing Wall' by Christians because of the mourning prayers Jews offered there, especially after being barred from Jerusalem during Byzantine rule
Israel gained control of the site during the Six-Day War on June 10, 1967, after it had been under Jordanian control since 1948
Visitors place written prayers into the cracks between the stones — a tradition dating to the 19th century
The Hebrew name 'Kotel' simply means 'wall'
items.cardFront: Jerusalem, Herod's retaining wall, holiest Jewish prayer site

items.prompt: Which city on the banks of the Ganges, one of the world's oldest continuously inhabited cities, is considered the spiritual capital of its faith and the holiest of its seven sacred cities?
items.explanation:
Holiest of the seven sacred cities in Hinduism, located on the western bank of the Ganges in Uttar Pradesh, India
One of the world's oldest continuously inhabited cities, with settlement dating to the 2nd millennium BCE
Regarded as the abode of Lord Shiva, the Hindu god of destruction and rebirth
Hindus believe dying in Varanasi grants moksha (liberation from the cycle of rebirth); cremation at Manikarnika Ghat is considered an instant pathway to liberation
Approximately 80 ghats (stone stairways) line the riverbank, used for ritual bathing, prayer, and cremation ceremonies
Historically also important for Buddhism — the Buddha delivered his first sermon at nearby Sarnath
Also known by its ancient names Kashi ('city of light') and Benares (anglicized from Banaras)
items.cardFront: Holiest Hindu city, Ganges, ghats, abode of Shiva

items.prompt: Which city at 11,975 feet elevation in the Himalayas served as the traditional seat of the Dalai Lama and houses the Potala Palace, a UNESCO World Heritage Site?
items.explanation:
Capital of the Tibet Autonomous Region, China, at ~12,000 feet elevation — one of the highest cities in the world
Traditional seat of the Dalai Lama, the spiritual leader of Tibetan Buddhism
Home to the Potala Palace, built beginning in 1645 by the Fifth Dalai Lama — served as the winter residence and seat of government until 1959
The Potala Palace is the highest palace in the world and became a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1994
Contains the White Palace (Potrang Karpo, government seat) and the Red Palace (Potrang Marpo, chapels and tombs of eight Dalai Lamas)
The Jokhang Temple, also in Lhasa, is considered the holiest temple in Tibetan Buddhism
The 14th Dalai Lama, Tenzin Gyatso, fled Lhasa in 1959 during the Tibetan uprising against Chinese rule and has lived in exile in Dharamsala, India, since
items.cardFront: Tibet, Potala Palace, Dalai Lama's seat, ~12,000 ft elevation
